points.headline: Thinking Machines ships Inkling, a 975B open-weights multimodal model.
points.body: Mira Murati's lab broke an 18-month silence with its first public model: Apache-2.0 licensed, trained to understand video and audio, and pitched on low cost plus resistance to censorship. Coverage frames it as the first credible American open-weights answer to Chinese labs, and as a direct jab at OpenAI, which has kept its frontier models closed.

points.headline: xAI open sources Grok Build days after it was caught uploading whole repos.
points.body: Users found that running the grok CLI in a directory silently shipped its contents, SSH keys and password vaults included, to xAI's cloud buckets. Musk promised a purge and the uploads stopped, though researchers say the privacy flag was not what actually fixed it; publishing the source is now the company's transparency gesture.

points.headline: The AI buildout is hitting political and physical limits.
points.body: New York became the first state to freeze construction of 50 MW-plus data centers for a year, Australia's PM proposed forcing AI companies to generate more energy than they consume, and a lawsuit accuses AWS of quietly guzzling water behind its "water positive" claims. The pattern: power, water, and land are now the binding constraints, and governments are the ones tightening them.

points.headline: AI is now both the attacker and the defender in security.
points.body: OpenAI revealed GPT-Red, an in-house LLM super-hacker used as a sparring partner to harden GPT-5.6 against prompt injection, while Microsoft credited AI for a record 570-plus Patch Tuesday fixes. On the other side, a researcher exfiltrated data through Claude's web_fetch tool and a jailbroken Gemini stood up a C2 server for a fraudster in six minutes.

points.headline: Enterprise agents remain mostly chatbots with better marketing.
points.body: A survey of 101 enterprises found orchestration consolidating on model-provider platforms, with Anthropic's Claude leading, but most deployed "agents" are still wrappers; Amazon's AGI autonomy director says reliability, not capability, explains why 85% pilot agents and only 5% ship. Stripe's new benchmark shows the same gap concretely: agents build integrations competently, then fail at validating them.

points.headline: Distribution, not benchmarks, is the new battleground.
points.body: Apple Intelligence won Chinese regulatory approval to launch on Alibaba's Qwen, Microsoft is reportedly coaching salespeople to pitch its in-house models as cheaper than OpenAI's and Anthropic's, and South Korea is tendering a free national chatbot built on local LLMs. Who gets to be the default model on a phone, a sales call, or a government service is where the value is settling.
